data.title: Algonquin Power & Utilities Non-GAAP EPS of $0.04 in-line
data.description: • Algonquin Power & Utilities press release (NYSE:AQN): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.04 in-line.

data.title: Embecta Non-GAAP EPS of $1.12 beats by $0.34, revenue of $295.5M beats by $17.35M
data.description:
• Embecta press release (NASDAQ:EMBC): Q3 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.12 beats by $0.34.
  • Revenue of $295.5M (+8.4% Y/Y) beats by $17.35M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA and margin of $131.0 million and 44.3%, compared to $99.2 million and 36.4% in the prior year period.
  • Announced a dividend of $0.15 per share.

data.title: RioCan Real Estate Investment Trust reports Q2 results
data.description:
• RioCan Real Estate Investment Trust press release (OTCPK:RIOCF): 9.3% growth of FFO per unit to $0.47.
  • Net income per unit of $0.49 was $0.08 per unit higher than the same period last year, reflecting greater fair value gains of $15.9 million on investment properties, compared to $5.9 million in the prior year quarter, in addition to the items noted for FFO above.

data.title: Lojas Renner S.A. GAAP EPS of R$0.40
data.description:
• Lojas Renner S.A. press release (OTCPK:LRENY): Q2 GAAP EPS of R$0.40.
  • Apparel sales increased by 20.0% and SSS by 18.6%, reaching a 58.4% gross margin (+0.9p.p.).
  • Retail gross margin increased by 0.9p.p., to 57.1%.
  • Youcom sales increased by 21.7%, achieving a gross margin of 63.7% (0.6p.p.).
  • Expense dilution of 0.8p.p; representing one more quarter of improvement.
  • Results for Realize CFI were R$ 118 MM (R$ 58 MM ex regulatory changes "Res. 4,966") and continued improvement in portfolio risk.
  • Total Adjusted EBITDA reached R$ 891 MM (+32.9%) with a 24.4% (+2.6p.p.) margin.
  • Cash position of R$ 1.8 bi and net cash position of R$ 1.2 bi.
  • Reduction of 12 days in the financial cycle and generation of R$ 333 MM in Free Cash Flow.
  • ~70% of the buyback program executed to date (52 million shares).

data.title: Gravity GAAP EPS of $1.41, revenue of $126.15M
data.description:
• Gravity press release (NASDAQ:GRVY): Q2 GAAP EPS of $1.41.
  • Revenue of $126.15M (+38.9% Y/Y).
  • Total revenues were KRW 170,740 million (US$ 126,147 thousand), representing a 24.2% increase from the first quarter ended March 31, 2025 (“QoQ”) and a 38.9% increase from the second quarter ended June 30, 2024 (“YoY”).

data.title: E-L Financial GAAP EPS of $1.07
data.description:
• E-L Financial press release (OTCPK:ELFIF): Q2 GAAP EPS of $1.07.
  • Net equity value per common share of $23.29 as at June 30, 2025, a decrease of 0.3% compared to $23.361 as at December 31, 2024 and an increase of 9.7% compared to $21.241 at June 30, 2024.

data.title: Hamilton signals continued double-digit premium growth with 18% Q2 increase amid leadership transitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hamilton Insurance Group (HG) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Giuseppina Carmela Albo, CEO, reported another profitable quarter and highlighted excellent underwriting results with a combined ratio of 86.8% and underwriting income of $67 million. She noted, “Investment income was also significant in this quarter at $149 million, reflecting strong returns from both the Two Sigma Hamilton Fund and our fixed income portfolio.” She announced net income of $187 million for the quarter, representing an annualized return on average equity of 30.2%.
  • Albo discussed recent management changes, including the retirement of Megan Graves as CEO of Hamilton Re, with Adrian Daws set to succeed her. Alex Baker will become CEO of Hamilton Global Specialty, and Tim Duffin will assume the newly created role of Group Chief Underwriting Officer in 2026. Additionally, Raymond Karrenbauer was appointed Group Chief Information Officer and Russ Buckley as Group Chief Risk Officer.
  • Albo emphasized strong top line growth, stating gross premiums written increased by 18% in the quarter, driven by Bermuda’s 26% growth and a $50 million contribution linked to an AM Best rating upgrade. She noted, “Bermuda led the way on growth, up 26%, driven predominantly by targeted casualty reinsurance business and new specialty reinsurance classes.”
  • The CEO also pointed to the International segment’s 11% gross premiums written growth and Hamilton Select’s 52% year-over-year advance.
  • Craig William Howie, CFO, remarked, “Hamilton had another strong quarter with net income of $187 million equal to $1.79 per diluted share, producing an annualized return on average equity of 30.2%.” He added, “We also increased book value per share by 8.3% this quarter to a record $25.55.”

Outlook

  • Management highlighted ongoing focus on disciplined underwriting and proactive cycle management, with Albo stating, “While the market may be coming off historic highs in some areas, there is no one market, and the key is to focus on rate adequacy.”
  • Albo indicated the company expects continued positive performance due to diversified platforms, a strong balance sheet, and robust broker relationships.
  • With the AM Best upgrade, management previously targeted $80 million in premiums for the year and reported $40 million in Q1 and $50 million in Q2, suggesting the company is ahead of schedule but expects more moderate growth ahead.

Financial Results

  • Hamilton delivered net income of $187 million and operating income of $162 million, or $1.55 per diluted share, with an annualized operating return on average equity of 26.1%.
  • Book value per share reached $25.55, an 11% increase from year-end 2024. Gross premiums written in the first half of 2025 increased to $1.6 billion from $1.3 billion in the prior year.
  • Group combined ratio was 86.8%, with International segment gross premiums written at $715 million (up 13%) and Bermuda segment at $841 million (up 21%).
  • Investment income totaled $149 million, driven by $87 million from the Two Sigma Hamilton Fund and $62 million from fixed income, short-term investments, and cash.
  • Share repurchases totaled $35 million in Q2, with an additional $15 million repurchased post-quarter, leaving $62 million authorized for future buybacks.

Q&A

  • Hristian Getsov, Wells Fargo: Asked about reserve increases in discontinued lines and accident years. CEO Albo responded the increase was modest and linked to business exited as part of a strategic transformation. CFO Howie specified, “This is $18 million. It’s a very manageable number for us… The years are 2020 and prior… We do not have a loss portfolio transfer with the Bermuda book.”
  • Getsov queried property pricing and exposure. Albo replied, “We did see some pressure on property D&S insurance… we were very disciplined there… The business we stayed on was probably midsized to smaller accounts.” She added, “Property pricing midyear was very deal specific, ranging from minus 15% to plus 50%.”
  • Unidentified Analyst (for BMO): Asked about MGA competition in Select. Albo responded, “We do not support MGAs in the Hamilton Select portfolio at all. We do all the underwriting in-house.”
  • On Bermuda casualty growth following the AM Best upgrade, Albo stated that $40 million was written in Q1 and $50 million in Q2, with future growth likely to moderate.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Asked about share repurchase run rate. Howie replied, “We buy back these shares for a couple of reasons. One is capital management, and the other is to take advantage of the share price because we think that the company is currently undervalued.”
  • Thomas Patrick McJoynt-Griffith, KBW: Inquired about premium growth headwinds from discontinued lines. Howie said, “We’re still expecting double-digit growth in premium, albeit at lower levels than what you’ve seen in the past.”
  • McJoynt-Griffith also asked about profit commission impact on the expense ratio. Howie explained, “It’s based on the underlying book of business… It could be in the International segment or it could be in the Bermuda segment depending on those lines. So it’s not a normal run rate.”
  • Getsov asked about reserve changes related to the Russia-Ukraine aviation losses. Howie confirmed, “This quarter, there was no change in our reserve for the quarter.”
  • Unidentified Analyst (BMO): Sought clarification on expense ratio components. Howie said, “The acquisition expense ratio is really based on mix of business… our margin improvement is in the other underwriting expenses. That ratio has declined each and every year since 2019.”
  • Matthew John Carletti, Citizens: Asked about tax rate. Howie responded, “We do not start paying global minimum tax until the year 2030. So our current effective tax rate is still in the low single digits.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in reserve development, premium growth, business mix, and share repurchases, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone and focused questioning.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing discipline and strength. Albo stated, “We are confident about continuing our positive performance trajectory.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was more upbeat, with both sides focusing on positive growth metrics and operational execution, while analyst tone remained constructive.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from caution around catastrophe losses in Q1 to a focus on proactive growth and successful underwriting in Q2.
  • Strategic focus moved from managing wildfire impacts and macroeconomic uncertainty to leveraging rating upgrades, talent, and disciplined underwriting.
  • Analysts in both quarters centered on reserve adequacy, premium growth, and expense ratios, but Q2 included more questions on share buybacks and leadership transitions.
  • Key metrics improved: net income rose from $81 million in Q1 to $187 million in Q2, book value per share increased, and combined ratio improved substantially.
  • Management displayed greater confidence in Q2, citing a strong balance sheet, ratings, and leadership transitions, while analysts appeared reassured by explanations of reserve and business mix changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged reserve increases in discontinued lines, emphasizing proactive cycle management and discipline.
  • Pressure in property pricing was observed, with selective participation in attractive segments.
  • Analysts flagged reserve adequacy, expense ratio drivers, and the sustainability of premium growth and share repurchases as key concerns.
  • Management addressed these by describing their reserving philosophy, business mix management, and capital adequacy for continued buybacks.

Final Takeaway

Hamilton Insurance Group delivered a strong second quarter marked by double-digit premium growth, robust underwriting income, and significant investment returns. Leadership transitions and recent rating upgrades were leveraged to drive new business and expand market opportunities, while the company maintained a disciplined approach to underwriting and capital management. The outlook remains positive, supported by a diversified platform, a growing balance sheet, and strategic focus on rate adequacy and talent development.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SmartCentres REIT reports Q2 results
data.description:
• SmartCentres REIT press release (OTCPK:CWYUF): Q2 net rental income and other for the three months ended June 30, 2025 was $141.3 million representing an increase of $8.1 million or 6.1% compared to the same period in 2024.
  • This increase was primarily due to lease-up and renewal activities mainly from retail properties.
  • FFO per unit for the three months ended June 30, 2025, was $0.58 compared to $0.50 for the same period in 2024.

data.title: Westrock Coffee affirms full-year 2025 guidance as Conway production ramps and adjusted EBITDA hits $23M in Q2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Westrock Coffee Company (WEST) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Scott Thomas Ford, Co-Founder and CEO, highlighted record-breaking second quarter results, stating, "We delivered record-breaking second quarter results driven by strong customer demand, improved margin performance and successful execution across our integrated platform this quarter." Ford emphasized the contribution of recent investments, saying, "Our progress towards our goal of becoming the premier integrated strategic supplier... has resulted in record production, record deliveries and record profits for our business this quarter."
  • Ford detailed performance drivers: "The beginning of commercial operations at the new single-serve cup plant along with production ramp-up at the extract and RTD plant in Conway, Arkansas, combined with cost controls... were the key drivers of this quarter's earnings beat."
  • Ford noted operational challenges, but added, "Our primary liquid product volumes are now approaching planned levels and we're closing in on our budgeted unit economics." He described the new single-serve cup facility's rapid growth: "We move from 3% of our total manufactured cups being made in this new plant to 25% in just 3 months."
  • Ford reaffirmed guidance, stating, "We are now again able to affirm our previous guidance for the back half and for the full year 2025."
  • CFO Thomas Christopher Pledger said, "On a consolidated basis, net sales increased by 34.8% compared to the second quarter of 2024." Pledger continued, "Although we reported a net loss of $21.6 million, this reflects our planned investment in the Conway extract and RTD facility."
  • Pledger provided additional detail: "Our consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $15.3 million which includes $7.6 million of scale-up operating costs related to the Conway facility."

Outlook

  • Pledger confirmed, "We had a very strong first half of the year and are pleased to report that our $23.6 million of consolidated adjusted EBITDA for the first half of 2025 put us at the top end of our guidance range."
  • Ford reiterated, "We are now again able to affirm our previous guidance for the back half and for the full year 2025."
  • Management acknowledged potential macroeconomic and consumer headwinds but said, "At this point, we see no reason to make any changes to our forward guidance."

Financial Results

  • Pledger stated, "Net sales increased by 34.8% compared to the second quarter of 2024."
  • The company reported a net loss of $21.6 million, attributed to "planned investment in the Conway extract and RTD facility."
  • Consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $15.3 million, including $7.6 million of Conway-related scale-up costs.
  • Beverage Solutions segment net sales increased 27.9% year-over-year, with segment adjusted EBITDA up 48.5% to $19.7 million.
  • The Sustainable Sourcing & Traceability (SS&T) segment posted net sales growth of 60% and segment adjusted EBITDA of $3.3 million.
  • Capital expenditures totaled approximately $20 million, primarily related to the Conway facility.
  • The company ended the quarter with $72 million in unrestricted cash and available liquidity under its $200 million revolving credit facility.

Q&A

  • Eric Des Lauriers, Craig-Hallum Capital Group: Asked for more context on results being "a bit different in mix" and the relationship with Palantir. Ford explained, "The mix was a little bit better in some of the categories... We were slower getting Conway started... but we made up for that in other areas in our core base business." On Palantir, Ford said, "The team that works with Palantir and the data... has simply been more powerful than we would have guessed."
  • Des Lauriers asked about the second Conway RTD can line. Ford responded, "It should be installed the middle of October in production early November."
  • William Bates Chappell, Truist Securities: Inquired about production visibility and room for upside. Ford stated, "We've got pretty good line of sight in terms of what we expect from customers that are in and running now... Other customers are scheduled to come on after the second can line comes in."
  • Chappell followed up on single-serve facility visibility. Pledger said, "The whole catalyst for it was winning new business... There's still capacity for us to be able to expand there."
  • Sarang Vora, Telsey Advisory Group: Asked about single-serve market share and future scale. Ford said, "Right now, I would say that we've filled out maybe 25% -- 20% of that space with machines, and the rest... is actually available for part of the distribution center." On tariffs, Ford explained, "There are some contracts we can say... we can get a reasonable profile of a bean from Colombia or Honduras or Rwanda or Uganda that can substitute for a 50% tariff... There is no flip of the switch."
  • Todd Morrison Brooks, The Benchmark Company: Asked about revenue capacity for Beverage Solutions. Pledger replied, "There is an opportunity to significantly grow revenue and grow EBITDA with the existing footprint that we have."
  • Brooks asked about cross-selling momentum. Ford responded, "Those top 20 accounts are driving all of the cross product enhancements that our business is really benefiting from."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, repeatedly congratulating management on strong results, production momentum, and facility ramp-ups.
  • Management tone in prepared remarks was confident and optimistic, with Ford stating, "I am so proud of this team. They are now just weeks away from pulling off what many said was impossible." During Q&A, management remained transparent and constructive, addressing operational challenges and business drivers directly.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, current sentiment from both analysts and management was more upbeat and focused on operational execution and forward momentum, while still acknowledging potential macroeconomic risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reported significantly higher net sales and adjusted EBITDA this quarter, with consolidated adjusted EBITDA reaching $15.3 million from $8.2 million in the previous quarter.
  • Guidance language remained consistent, with management affirming full-year and second-half targets in both quarters.
  • Focus shifted from discussing the initial launch and ramp of Conway facilities in Q1 to reporting record production and improving unit economics in Q2.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on capacity, production ramp, and customer demand, but the Q2 call included more detailed discussion of segment performance and operational execution.
  • Management confidence increased, with stronger emphasis on the role of Palantir and efficiency gains in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Pledger highlighted the impact of tariffs, stating, "Tariffs remain a significant focus across the industry and will likely reflect a new normal for our operating environment going forward."
  • The company noted that tariffs on Brazilian imports "impact the value of our inventory and as a result place additional pressure on our working capital and liquidity in the short term."
  • Management identified potential headwinds from "elevated tariffs, persistent inflation and any softening in consumer confidence."
  • Mitigation strategies include leveraging green coffee inventory, passing through tariff costs to customers, and maintaining liquidity through available credit lines.

Final Takeaway

Westrock Coffee Company delivered record quarterly results, driven by strong demand, margin improvement, and successful scale-up of its Conway facilities. The company affirmed full-year 2025 guidance, highlighted progress in production ramp and cross-selling wins, and maintained a confident outlook despite acknowledging ongoing macroeconomic and tariff-related risks. Management underscored operational resilience, segment growth, and robust liquidity as key strengths supporting continued momentum into the second half of the year.
